title: Cohomological Automorphic Representations on Unitary Groups
speaker: Rahul Dalal
abstract: Consider the family of automorphic representations on some unitary group with fixed (possibly non-tempered) cohomological representation π0 at infinity and level dividing some finite upper bound. We compute statistics of this family as the level restriction goes to infinity. For unramified unitary groups and a large class of π0, we are able to compute the exact leading term for both counts of representations and averages of Satake parameters. We get bounds on our error term similar to previous work by Shin-Templier that studied the case of discrete series at infinity.

proof_stages.label: Counting problem and infinity-type hierarchy
proof_stages.summary: The talk reduces applications of automorphic representations to counting discrete automorphic representations satisfying local restrictions, emphasizing that the tractable boundary is the cohomological range at infinity.

proof_stages.label: Exact asymptotic theorem for good cohomological infinity type
proof_stages.summary: For an unramified CM extension and split level on an inner form of a unitary group, the main theorem gives an explicit leading term and power-saving error for the weighted count of automorphic representations with fixed cohomological component pi_0 at infinity, subject to a combinatorial goodness condition.

proof_stages.label: Upper bounds and expected consequences
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states a more general ramified upper-bound theorem with an auxiliary positive weighting function and explains intended corollaries such as Sato-Tate equidistribution, low-lying zeros, Sarnak-Xue density bounds, and cohomology growth via Matsushima's formula.

proof_stages.label: Endoscopic classification framework
proof_stages.summary: Arthur-Mok-Kaletha-Minguez-Shin-White classification is recalled as a partition of the discrete automorphic spectrum into packets indexed by elliptic Arthur parameters, together with stabilization and the stable multiplicity formula relating packet traces to twisted GL_N traces.

proof_stages.label: Adams-Johnson packets and pseudocoefficient isolation
proof_stages.summary: Cohomological representations at infinity are identified with Adams-Johnson packets, and a pseudocoefficient is chosen that isolates the desired pi_0 among representations sharing its archimedean Arthur packet.

proof_stages.label: Refined shapes and shape-restricted trace formula
proof_stages.summary: The proof groups Arthur parameters by refined shape, uses the Adams-Johnson shape set attached to pi_0 to express the desired count as a sum of shape-restricted invariant trace terms, and then switches to stable trace terms by stabilization and hyperendoscopy.

proof_stages.label: Inductive transfer reduction and dominant-shape computation
proof_stages.summary: An induction reduces stable trace terms for complicated shapes to products of trivial-shape terms through stable transfer and Speh transfer, uses Shin-Templier bounds at the base, and in the split unramified unitary setting computes only the dominant transfers while absorbing the rest into the error term.

examples.name: GL2 over Q as modular and Maass forms
examples.note: The speaker uses classical modular forms and Maass forms for GL2/Q to make the hierarchy discrete-at-infinity/cohomological/algebraic/general concrete; no new idea is derived from it.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses GL2 over Q to list which modular/Maass forms sit at each complexity level, without deriving or computing anything on GL2

passes.reason: GL2 over Q is used only as a standard illustrative case connecting automorphic representations with modular and Maass forms.

examples.name: Parallel U(n-1,1)^d special case
examples.note: The speaker spells out the theorem's constants for U(n-1,1)^d to illustrate the combinatorics of the good condition, the exponent r_pi0, and the possible vanishing of the leading constant.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes to parallel U(n-1,1)^d, describes the partition/marked-block classification, states the good condition, formulas for r and c, and reasons through dependence on m and parity

passes.reason: The U(n−1,1)^d parallel case has rank-one boundary signature at each infinite place and a maximally regular parallel choice of representation.

examples.name: Rogawski unit-ball quotients with persistent Betti vanishing
examples.note: An audience question recalls Rogawski's U(3) examples where congruence covers do not switch on first cohomology, illustrating how zero constants and Matsushima-type consequences might produce geometric vanishing applications.
examples.engagement_reason: Rogawski’s unit-ball quotient example is cited in a question as motivation for possible Betti-vanishing applications, but the talk does not analyze that example

passes.reason: Rogawski’s ball quotients have the minimal possible first Betti number throughout congruence towers and are cited against the expectation that cohomology eventually switches on.
